FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study participants (N=640 adolescents from ABCD Study years 4, 5, 6)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone app-category use (time on communication, social, gaming, entertainment apps); specifically Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat. B1 applies: time on phone (EARS passive sensing) with per-app breakdown including app categories.

FT_IC3.evidence: Descriptive statistics of app usage with adjusted means calculated (t-tests implied in emmeans package usage). Describes smartphone use patterns by demographic categories.

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted means with 95% CI reported (e.g., mean daily screen time 6.1±5.2 hours). Numeric extractable data in main paper and supplements.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis using EARS passive sensing data from years 4-6

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research; no indication of duplicate/preprint

FT_EC7_reason: Paper describes smartphone USE (as IV), not as dependent variable. Shows smartphone -> outcome associations (smartphone time to app-specific descriptors), not demographic-only predictors of smartphone. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (a) because SM is IV, not DV only.
notes: Supplementary online content reviewed. Focuses on characterizing school-day smartphone app usage patterns. Primary outcome is descriptive characterization of app use, not inferential SM↔outcome analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: 5356 US adolescents in ABCD Study Years 2 (2018-2020, ages 11-12) and Year 4 (2020-2022, ages 13-14)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time only (no per-item breakdown of platform/app/content). Reports total daily screen time (6.1±5.2 h/day) without distinguishing smartphone, tablet, TV, or gaming platform. B2 applies: summed/total screen time with no per-item breakdown.

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective regression analysis: Poisson regression models and multivariable linear regression examining screen time → BMI/overweight-obesity risk. Inferential testing of screen time ↔ BMI outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: Risk ratios (RR) with 95% CI and p-values: high screen time RR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02-1.16, p=0.013); coefficient B=3.27 (95% CI 1.54-4.99, p<0.001); RR 1.23 (95% CI 1.07-1.40, p=0.003)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis of ABCD data examining screen time associations with BMI outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research; no duplicate indication

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time is IV (independent variable), not DV. Analysis is screen time → BMI outcome, not demographic → screen time.
notes: Critical limitation: Screen time is unspecified-platform/device aggregate (TV + console + devices without breakdown). Does not qualify as smartphone/SM variable per B5, B7 rules.

FT_IC1.evidence: 7407 US adolescents from ABCD Study years 3-5 (2019-2021 to 2021-2023)

FT_IC2.evidence: Video game use only (gaming without social media or smartphone time). No qualifying SM variable (time on social media, platforms, etc.). No separate SM measure. B6 inapplicable: gaming alone without SM coefficient/separate measurement. Per B5, unspecified gaming (without SM) does not meet criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple prospective mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models examining parenting practices → video game behaviors 1 and 2 years later. Inferential testing present.

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% CI and p-values reported (e.g., associations for mature-rated games, problematic use, weekend gaming time with quantified estimates)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis of ABCD data examining parenting practices → video game outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original research; no duplicate indication

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET (no qualifying SM). Additionally, predictors include parenting practices (NOT in demographic-only closed set per B17: parenting disqualifies EC7).
notes: Video gaming is unspecified-platform gaming not paired with SM measure. Parenting practices (screen modeling, monitoring, limiting, mealtime/bedroom use) are explicitly excluded from demographic-only predictors. Does not meet inclusion criteria.
